Name: kernel_optimization_throughput_enhancement
Title: Profiling-Driven Kernel Optimization for Latency-Aware Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging fine-grained profiling data to inform kernel fusion and memory access patterns, we hypothesize that we can achieve significant reductions in wall-clock latency and improvements in throughput for existing machine learning inference tasks on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: Existing literature, such as TritonForge, focuses on automated kernel optimization but often lacks specificity in profiling-driven adaptations for real-world workloads. Our approach distinguishes itself by emphasizing empirical evaluations using detailed performance metrics, including occupancy and memory bandwidth utilization, which are often not adequately addressed in automated frameworks.
Abstract: In this proposal, we present a novel approach to GPU kernel optimization aimed at enhancing the latency and throughput of machine learning inference tasks on commodity GPUs. We hypothesize that detailed profiling data can be effectively utilized to inform kernel fusion strategies and optimize memory access patterns. Our method will focus on measuring key performance indicators such as occupancy, achieved bandwidth, and warp stall reasons through rigorous profiling. We will implement targeted kernel modifications based on empirical insights gained from profiling data, aiming to minimize wall-clock latency and maximize tokens-per-second metrics. The proposed experiments will evaluate the efficacy of our approach across various machine learning models, providing a reproducible benchmark for the research community. By focusing on practical, hardware-faithful optimizations, we aim to contribute a straightforward yet impactful framework for kernel optimization that prioritizes measurable gains.
Experiments: 1. Benchmarking existing kernels using nvprof and Nsight to establish a baseline performance profile.
2. Implementing targeted kernel fusion strategies based on profiling insights to reduce memory access latency.
3. Evaluating memory access patterns and redesigning data structures for optimal cache utilization.
4. Conducting A/B latency races to compare pre- and post-optimization performance, measuring p50/p99 latency and throughput under varying batch sizes.
5. Documenting energy consumption and performance metrics to assess the trade-offs involved.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The optimizations may be hardware-specific, limiting generalizability across diverse GPU architectures.
2. Profiling overhead could affect the real-time performance metrics, potentially skewing results.
3. The complexity of kernel fusion may lead to diminishing returns if not paired with careful profiling.
